title: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26
wikitext:
[]: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 20, 2010 1:29 pm This thread is for the discussion of two Episodes in tandem with two sociologists. Episodes 25 and 26 each sport a story line based around sociological theory from two sociologists, Charles Horton Cooley and George Herbert Mead. Episode 25 worked around Charles Horton Cooley, mainly his theory of "The Looking Glass Self", in which he proposes that we learn who we are by interacting with others. An example would be Shinji conversing with himself in his mind, and the "himself" that existed in everyone elses mind. Episode 26 works with a work meant to expand upon "The Looking Glass Self", a work produced by George Herbert Mead. Mead expanded to the concept of the "I" and the "Me". The "I" is the acting self and the "Me" is our socialized self. The part this episode foucused on however was his theory of the process of sociological development. Mead proposed three stages, which I shall list with the definition and an example from the episode. First is the Preparatory stage. This is the stage in life when chirldren begin to imitate their elders. They skipped this step though because Shinji has already gone through that stage in his life. The second stage, and the first anno expands on really in episode 26 is the Play Stage. During this stage of life, chirldren role-play adult positions. In episode 26 though, instead of role-playing an adult position, he is instead made to role-play a day in his life if the Evangelion had never existed. The last stage is the Game Stage where chirldren begin to realise and act upon their sociological position. This can be seen by shini's final acceptance of everything he had just learned, and the following scene where his family and friends congradulate him. Thats as much as I have in me for today, so i'll leave you fine folks to discuss this, and I suggest as well that you do research on those names so you are familiar with their theories. Here are links: Charles Horton Cooley [url]http://www.d.umn.edu/cla/faculty/jhamlin/2111/Blumer/Charles%20Horton%20Cooley%20-%20Looking%20Glass%20Self.htm[/url] George Herbert Mead [url]http://www.marxists.org/reference/subject/philosophy/works/us/mead3.htm[/url] Enjoy![/url]

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by Allemann » Tue Apr 20, 2010 1:39 pm supershinjiasukashipper wrote: Episodes 25 and 26 each sport a story line based around sociological theory from two sociologists, Charles Horton Cooley and George Herbert Mead.

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by Merridian » Tue Apr 20, 2010 1:44 pm Makes sense, but most of this is covered with the existentialist angle(s) that has/have been beaten around the intellectualz already, though. supershinjiasukashipper wrote: The second stage, and the first anno expands on really in episode 26 is the Play Stage. During this stage of life, chirldren role-play adult positions. In episode 26 though, instead of role-playing an adult position, he is instead made to role-play a day in his life if the Evangelion had never existed.

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 20, 2010 1:53 pm Allemann wrote: Has Anno somewhere mentioned that these sociologists influenced him, or is this your personal conjecture?

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by Allemann » Tue Apr 20, 2010 1:58 pm supershinjiasukashipper wrote: Personal conjecture actually, but after you read their work on these particular theories, i'ts hard not to say that they had to, it's a mirror image of their work, which is why I loved those episodes so much even though I prefer EoE.

[]: Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:03 pm SSAS wrote: Personal conjecture actually, but after you read their work on these particular theories, i'ts hard not to say that they had to, it's a mirror image of their work, which is why I loved those episodes so much even though I prefer EoE.

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:06 pm Allemann wrote: A discussion based on a unsubstantiated personal conjecture is a time waster and can lead to a pointless dead end.

[]: Post by Allemann » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:07 pm ran1 wrote: 19th century Existentialists beat you to it. A lot of the sociologist just took Kierkegaard and the other philosophers and made their societal theories a bit more empirical, thus Sociology. However, blame people like Max Weber for that method.

[]: Post by ran1 » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:09 pm critiquing it and stimulating it to change.

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by Allemann » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:11 pm supershinjiasukashipper wrote: Well the main point for this track of discussion is to discuss these apparent parrallels.

[]: Re: Sociology in Episodes 25 and 26 Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 20, 2010 2:16 pm Allemann wrote: They're not apparent as you think they are.

[]: Post by Merridian » Tue Apr 20, 2010 4:06 pm SSAS wrote: a far streatch, but it works when you ponder it under the assumption that after instrumentality he would return to normal which is all we can conjecture from that ending really without making it completely gloomy.

[]: Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Tue Apr 20, 2010 4:09 pm Merridian wrote: far stretch indeed. Especially considering that the only people shown at the end were the equally dysfunctional Asuka & Shinji Sociology is just the study of individuals in groups (/society). Critique/stimulation is more in the territory of social commentary/criticism, which can be incorporated into anything from philosophy & art to journalism

[]: Post by chaosakita » Tue Apr 20, 2010 9:00 pm Did you ever think that it could just be...a coincidence?

[]: Post by skikes » Tue Apr 20, 2010 9:14 pm What is this? I don't even... Do you even know what sociology is? Do you know what a sociopath is? I suggest you goggle it. Tell me what are your thoughts on mixology present througout Eva?

[]: Post by CorporalChaos » Tue Apr 20, 2010 9:16 pm I'm more convinced that Harlan Ellison's "The Beast that Shouted 'Love' at the Heart of the World" has a bigger effect on the story-telling style. By the sounds of it, the actual dialogue is influenced by these two books, but the actual storytelling itself is more reminiscent of Ellison's story. In it, the story is told from differing points of view. These stories are supposed to be connected by spokes running through a common theme, and that common theme is the true story. It's certainly interesting to read about the sources of the ideas, though, and to see how those ideas get blended together to make a really unique final product.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Apr 20, 2010 11:11 pm ran1 wrote: Not sure if it was as hopeful as you claim. It was emotional, but I'm not sure hopeful is the right word for it.

[]: Post by supershinjiasukashipper » Wed Apr 21, 2010 2:18 pm Eva Yojimbo wrote: Not to go off on a tangent here, but I there there is definitely a glimmer of hope in that final scene; particularly in Asuka's caress and the role-reversal displayed by both.

[]: Post by Allemann » Wed Apr 21, 2010 2:26 pm supershinjiasukashipper wrote: Either way, somewhere along the line you have to admit there is a good chance that these works did their dirty work at the crossroads.

[]: Post by ran1 » Wed Apr 21, 2010 2:31 pm I would be much more willing to accept parts of your theory if you could link any part of the sources that you are referencing. That I way I could read them and full and evaluate the primary sources that you are utilizing. Just throwing out a few names doesn't help much.
